metadata.summary: Spring @Async with ThreadPoolTaskExecutor for running methods on managed thread pools without blocking HTTP threads. Named executors, CompletableFuture returns, TaskDecorator context propagation.

content.title: Spring Async
content.summary: Spring's @Async + ThreadPoolTaskExecutor pattern for running methods on a managed thread pool without blocking the HTTP thread. Annotate a method @Async("executorName"), enable with @EnableAsync, define a ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean, and return CompletableFuture<T> so callers can observe errors. The async method must live in a different bean than the caller to avoid proxy self-invocation bypass.

why.p: Offloading fire-and-forget work (email, SMS, audit logging, webhook delivery) to a named pool removes it from the request thread, reducing p99 latency. Named executors with bounded queues and CallerRunsPolicy provide backpressure without dropping tasks. Unlike raw ExecutorService, Spring-managed pools participate in DI, observability (Micrometer metrics), graceful shutdown, and context propagation (MDC, SecurityContext, OTel span) via TaskDecorator.

list.item: Background tasks that must not block HTTP threads: email/SMS, CRM sync, report generation, image transcoding.
Fan-out: controller fires multiple downstream calls, aggregates via CompletableFuture.allOf.
Event-driven side effects using ApplicationEventPublisher + @Async @EventListener.
Migrating raw ExecutorService boilerplate to Spring-managed pools with proper shutdown and metrics.
Fire-and-forget audit logging or metrics that must not impact request latency.
Cross-process work needing durability, retries, or idempotency — use SQS / RabbitMQ / Kafka instead; @Async does not survive a JVM restart.
High-throughput streaming — use Project Reactor / WebFlux end-to-end; mixing @Async with reactive code wastes threads.
Latency-sensitive paths where work MUST complete before response — use synchronous code.
Spring Boot 3.2+ apps with virtual threads enabled — @Async adds no value and may interfere with Loom schedulers.
Exactly-once semantics, durable state, or saga workflows — use Temporal or a queue broker.

section.name: executor-configuration
section.p: Rules for creating named ThreadPoolTaskExecutor beans with proper shutdown, backpressure, and context propagation via TaskDecorator.

rule.statement: Define a named ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean and always reference it by name: @Async("taskExecutor"). Never use bare @Async — it falls back to SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor (unbounded threads, no pooling).
rule.rationale: SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor creates a new thread per call. Under load this exhausts OS thread limits silently. A named bounded pool provides predictable resource usage.

rule.statement: Set corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, queueCapacity, threadNamePrefix, and rejectionPolicy on every ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean. Set setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true) and setAwaitTerminationSeconds to a bounded value.
rule.rationale: Without shutdown wait, in-flight tasks (emails, webhook calls) are dropped on JVM shutdown during deploys. Bounded termination prevents graceful shutdown from hanging indefinitely.

rule.statement: Use CallerRunsPolicy as the default rejection policy for fire-and-forget executors. Use AbortPolicy only when the caller can handle RejectedExecutionException.
rule.rationale: CallerRunsPolicy degrades gracefully under overload by running the task on the caller's thread, providing natural backpressure without dropping tasks.

rule.statement: Wrap every ThreadPoolTaskExecutor in a TaskDecorator that copies MDC context map, SecurityContextHolder, and OTel current span to the async thread before execution, restoring originals afterward.
rule.rationale: MDC (trace IDs), SecurityContext (authenticated principal), and OTel span are ThreadLocal. Without propagation, async threads produce untraced logs and null principals, breaking observability and security.

example.title: AsyncConfig with TaskDecorator
example.note: One @EnableAsync per application (in a @Configuration class). Multiple executor beans are allowed — name each descriptively (emailExecutor, reportExecutor).

rule.statement: Register Micrometer metrics on every ThreadPoolTaskExecutor: queued, active, completed, rejected counts. Alert on rejected > 0 for any non-CallerRuns pool.
rule.rationale: Without metrics, pool exhaustion is invisible until the application degrades. rejected > 0 is an immediate operational signal.

section.name: method-annotation-rules
section.p: Rules for annotating async methods, returning CompletableFuture for error observability, and composing fan-out calls.

rule.statement: The @Async method must live in a different Spring bean than the caller. If the caller and the target are in the same class, extract the async method to a dedicated @Service (e.g., AsyncEmailService).
rule.rationale: @Async works via a Spring proxy. Calling this.asyncMethod() inside the same bean bypasses the proxy — the method runs synchronously and silently. This is the #1 cause of @Async "not working" bug reports.

rule.statement: Return CompletableFuture<Void> (or CompletableFuture<T>) from @Async methods where errors must be observable. For true fire-and-forget, wire an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler in AsyncConfig that logs the error and increments a Prometheus counter.
rule.rationale: void @Async methods drop exceptions silently unless an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler is registered. Lost exceptions mean lost operational visibility.

rule.statement: Do not place @Transactional and @Async on the same method. An async method runs in a separate thread, so the caller's transaction is not propagated. Either the caller opens no transaction, or the async method opens its own.
rule.rationale: @Transactional binds a connection to the calling thread. The async thread is a different thread — it gets no connection from the caller's tx. The code compiles and appears to work in tests (synchronous by default) but fails in production.

example.title: NotificationService with fan-out
example.note: NotificationService is a separate bean from any controller that calls it — proxy interception works correctly.

rule.statement: Test @Async methods with Awaitility (await().atMost(2, SECONDS).untilAsserted(...)). Never use Thread.sleep.
rule.rationale: Thread.sleep is a fixed wait that produces flaky tests: too short on slow CI, too long for developer feedback. Awaitility polls until the condition is true or the timeout expires.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Concrete failure modes that agents introduce when adding @Async; each has a detectable symptom and a required fix.

antipattern.description: Self-invocation: calling this.asyncMethod() inside the same @Service class.
antipattern.reason: Spring's @Async proxy is not engaged for internal method calls. The method runs synchronously on the caller's thread with no pooling, no error routing, no context propagation. The bug is silent — no exception is thrown, the method just blocks.

antipattern.description: Bare @Async without a named executor: @Async with no argument or @Async("").
antipattern.reason: Falls back to SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor, which creates a new OS thread per invocation. Under sustained load, thread count grows without bound until the JVM or OS kills the process. No backpressure, no metrics, no pool reuse.

antipattern.description: void-returning @Async method with no AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler registered in AsyncConfig.
antipattern.reason: Exceptions thrown by void @Async methods are silently discarded. Failures (email send errors, webhook rejections) produce no log, no metric, no alert. Operators have no visibility.

antipattern.description: @Transactional and @Async on the same method, expecting the caller's transaction to propagate.
antipattern.reason: The async thread is a different OS thread. Spring cannot propagate a JDBC connection (bound to a ThreadLocal) across threads. The async method reads uncommitted data or opens its own unrelated transaction.

On JVM shutdown (during deploys), in-flight async tasks are killed immediately. Email sends, webhook deliveries, and audit log writes are silently dropped. Durable work must use a queue, but at minimum the shutdown hook must drain the in-flight pool.

antipattern.description: Thread.sleep() in tests waiting for async completion.
antipattern.reason: Produces flaky tests: too short on slow CI, too long on fast hardware. Use Awaitility's await().atMost().untilAsserted() which polls until the condition is met or times out.

antipattern.description: Using @Async for work that requires durability: charging cards, sending invoices, triggering external state transitions.
antipattern.reason: @Async tasks live in JVM memory. A JVM restart (crash, OOM, deploy) permanently loses in-flight tasks. Durable work requires a persistent queue (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) with at-least-once delivery.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Recommended agent-driven patterns for adopting Spring Async in existing applications.

Drive Spring Async adoption as a four-stage pipeline: (1) a discovery agent enumerates candidate methods (long-running, no return value needed by caller, idempotent on retry); (2) a config agent verifies / creates ThreadPoolTaskExecutor beans with TaskDecorator for MDC + Security + OTel context; (3) a refactor agent annotates methods, replaces void with CompletableFuture<Void> where errors must be observable, splits async methods to a separate bean to avoid self-invocation; (4) a test agent generates Awaitility + WireMock-based tests. Persist async-eligible inventory in .aidocs/product_docs/async-inventory.md. Use faion-sdd-executor-agent to drive each method per SDD task.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — opus model fits because async/sync trade-offs (consistency, retries, backpressure) are decision-heavy.
faion-feature-executor skill — sequential mode: config → refactor → test → integration test, gating on green.

A purpose-built async-anti-pattern agent: linter for self-invocation, missing @EnableAsync, void-returning @Async without AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler, @Async on method that calls JpaRepository.save inside a parent @Transactional.
password-scrubber-agent — async tasks frequently log payload bodies (email content, webhook bodies) that contain PII / tokens; scrub before commit.

section.prompt: You are converting a method to Spring @Async on Spring Boot 3.4. Constraints: 1. The async method MUST live in a different bean than the caller (no self-invocation). If currently same bean, extract to a new @Service. 2. Annotate the class @EnableAsync once (in @Configuration). Reuse existing AsyncConfig if present. 3. Use a named executor: @Async("taskExecutor") or "emailExecutor". Bean must be ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, queueCapacity, threadNamePrefix, CallerRunsPolicy, and setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true). 4. Wrap with TaskDecorator that copies MDC + SecurityContextHolder + OTel current span. 5. If errors matter, return CompletableFuture<Void> and add .exceptionally to the caller; OR set AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler in AsyncConfig that logs + increments a Prometheus counter. 6. NEVER call the @Async method from a parent @Transactional expecting transaction propagation; remove the parent @Transactional or split. 7. Test with Awaitility, never Thread.sleep.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Command-line tools for async workload profiling and testing.

list.item: mvn / gradle — Build, test, run. https://maven.apache.org , https://gradle.org
Spring Boot Actuator + /actuator/threaddump — Inspect live thread pools. https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/current/reference/html/actuator.html
jcmd (pid) Thread.print — JVM thread dump from CLI. https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/docs/specs/man/jcmd.html
async-profiler — Sample async / blocking time. https://github.com/async-profiler/async-profiler
Micrometer + Prometheus — executor.queued, executor.active, executor.completed metrics. https://micrometer.io
awaitility — Test fluent waits. org.awaitility:awaitility ; https://github.com/awaitility/awaitility
WireMock CLI — Stub external HTTP for async integration tests. https://wiremock.org
jstack / jfr (Java Flight Recorder) — Profiling thread / lock contention. bundled with JDK

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: SaaS and OSS tools for durability, observability, and scaling beyond @Async.

list.item: RabbitMQ + Spring AMQP (OSS) — Use when @Async outgrows in-process (durability, retries).
Apache Kafka + Spring Cloud Stream (OSS) — High-throughput async; replace @Async for event fan-out.
AWS SQS + spring-cloud-aws (SaaS) — Drop-in cloud queue.
Temporal (OSS / Cloud) — Where async needs durable state machines / saga.
Redis Streams + Spring Data Redis (OSS) — Cheap pub/sub upgrade path.
Hazelcast / Ignite distributed executors (OSS) — Cluster-wide async work.
Sentry / Datadog APM (SaaS) — Observe async tasks; trace propagation requires TaskDecorator.
OpenTelemetry Java agent (OSS) — Auto-instrumentation propagates async context if executor is wrapped.
Spring Batch (OSS) — When async grows into chunked jobs with restart semantics.
Quartz Scheduler (OSS) — Cron-like scheduled @Async; Boot integration via spring-boot-starter-quartz.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Proven patterns and practices for Spring Async applications.

list.item: Async method lives in a different bean than the caller. No self-invocation. Refactor into a dedicated AsyncEmailService if needed.
Always use a named executor. @Async("taskExecutor") — not just @Async. Default is unbounded SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor.
ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with explicit limits. Core, max, queue, prefix, rejection policy, shutdown wait. README's AsyncConfig is a good baseline; copy it.
TaskDecorator propagates context. MDC (logging trace IDs), SecurityContext, OTel current span, request locale. Without this, observability is broken.
Return CompletableFuture<T> if errors must be observable. void async swallows exceptions unless AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler is wired.
Don't share @Transactional across async boundary. Either the parent doesn't open a tx, or the async method opens its own (and read-after-commit is needed).
Backpressure-friendly rejection policy. CallerRunsPolicy for fire-and-forget where degradation is OK; AbortPolicy only when caller can handle the exception.
Metrics on every executor. Micrometer exports executor.queued, active, completed, rejected. Alert on rejected > 0 for any non-CallerRuns pool.
setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true) + bounded setAwaitTerminationSeconds. No silent task loss on deploy.
Virtual threads on Boot 3.2+ where applicable. spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true lets the platform handle scheduling; revisit per-pool sizing.
Test with Awaitility, not Thread.sleep. await().atMost(2, SECONDS).untilAsserted(...).
Boundaries with external systems = queue, not @Async. Email vendor down → @Async retries die with the JVM. Use a queue for durability.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common mistakes agents introduce when adding Spring Async.

list.item: Self-invocation reflex. Agents add @Async to a method and call it from the same class. The proxy doesn't intercept and the method runs synchronously. Force the rule: "If the caller and @Async method are in the same bean, refactor."
Wrong executor. Agents leave @Async empty and code uses SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor (unbounded threads). Force named executor + bean definition.
Lost SecurityContext. Agents call SecurityContextHolder.getContext() inside the async method — null. Add DelegatingSecurityContextAsyncTaskExecutor or TaskDecorator.
@Async + @Transactional on same method. Tx applied to the caller's thread, not the async thread. Misleading. Reject in review.
Returning void and losing exceptions. Agents do not wire AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler. Force one in AsyncConfig that logs + emits a metric.
@Async for things that need durability. Agents use @Async for charging cards / sending invoices. JVM restart loses the work. Force a queue + handler.
Thread.sleep in tests. Agents test async by Thread.sleep(500); assertX(). Flaky. Use Awaitility + untilAsserted.
MDC drop. Agents log inside async method, lose trace ID, and debugging is dark. Add TaskDecorator that copies MDC.getCopyOfContextMap().
Default rejection policy crashes user requests. Default AbortPolicy throws RejectedExecutionException to caller. Pick CallerRunsPolicy for fire-and-forget OR a dedicated handler that logs + queues.
Virtual-thread confusion. Agents wire @Async AND virtual threads, doubling abstraction. Decide one model per pool.

Memory leak via unbounded CompletableFuture chains. Agents chain .thenApplyAsync(...).thenApplyAsync(...) indefinitely; without explicit executors, the common pool runs everything. Pin executor on each *Async.
Human-in-the-loop on parallelism numbers. corePoolSize / maxPoolSize / queueCapacity are tuning decisions; never let an agent guess. Require benchmark + load test before merge.

Observability gaps. Agents add @Async and forget to register Micrometer metrics on the executor (Metrics.gauge("executor.active", taskExecutor, ThreadPoolTaskExecutor::getActiveCount)). Force registration in the config.
